tier_1_fundamentals.title: Hypertrophy Fundamentals
tier_1_fundamentals.description: Essential mechanisms of muscle growth. Passing this proves you understand how to stimulate tissue.

questions.question: Which of the following is the PRIMARY driver of muscle hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Mechanical Tension is the force generated by muscle fibers. Without it, growth signals are weak.

questions.question: According to the Repetition Continuum, which rep range can build muscle?
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy is similar across 5-30 reps IF sets are taken close to failure.

questions.question: What is Progressive Overload?
questions.explanation: You must increase the stimulus (weight, reps, or sets) over time to force adaptation.

options.text: Changing exercises every week to 'confuse' the muscle
Doing the exact same workout until it feels easy

questions.question: How much protein is generally recommended for maximizing hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: 1.6g/kg to 2.2g/kg is the scientific gold standard (Morton et al.).

questions.question: Why is sleep critical for hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Growth Hormone release and neural recovery occur predominantly during deep and REM sleep.

options.text: It is when protein synthesis and neural recovery peak
It isn't; you can grow on 4 hours

questions.question: What is the optimal training frequency per muscle group for most natural lifters?
questions.explanation: 2-3x per week allows for higher quality volume and spikes MPS more often than 1x.

questions.question: What defines a 'Compound' exercise?
questions.explanation: Multi-joint movements recruit more muscle mass and allow for heavier loading.

questions.question: True or False: You must be sore after a workout to grow muscle.
questions.explanation: Soreness (DOMS) is a sign of damage/novelty, not necessarily growth. It often competes with recovery.

questions.question: In the context of dieting, what is a 'Caloric Surplus'?
questions.explanation: Eating more energy than you burn provides the raw materials for new tissue construction.

options.text: Eating fewer calories than you burn
Eating more calories than you burn

questions.question: What is RIR (Reps In Reserve)?
questions.explanation: RIR is a measure of proximity to failure. 2 RIR means you could have done 2 more reps with good form.

options.text: How many reps you have left before failure

tier_2_intermediate.title: Intermediate Programming
tier_2_intermediate.description: Fatigue management, effective reps, and stimulus-to-fatigue ratios.

questions.question: What are 'Effective Reps'?
questions.explanation: The last ~5 reps before failure where motor unit recruitment is maximal due to the size principle.

options.text: The first 5 reps of a set
The reps close to failure that recruit high-threshold motor units

questions.question: What is the concept of 'Stimulus to Fatigue Ratio' (SFR)?
questions.explanation: We want exercises that provide high tension (Stimulus) with low joint stress/systemic burnout (Fatigue).

options.text: Maximizing growth signal while minimizing systemic cost
Lifting as heavy as possible regardless of form

questions.question: Why might training a muscle in the 'Lengthened Position' be superior?
questions.explanation: Stretch-mediated hypertrophy suggests tension at long muscle lengths produces more growth (e.g., seated leg curl vs lying).

options.text: It creates greater mechanical tension and IGF-1 release

questions.question: What is a 'Deload'?
questions.explanation: A planned reduction in volume/intensity to drop fatigue while retaining fitness.

options.text: A planned week of reduced volume to dissipate fatigue

questions.question: What is MEV (Minimum Effective Volume)?
questions.explanation: The lowest amount of volume needed to make measurable progress.

options.text: The most you can recover from
The minimum volume to maintain size
The minimum volume to stimulate growth

questions.question: What is MRV (Maximum Recoverable Volume)?
questions.explanation: The volume ceiling. Exceeding this leads to overreaching and regression.

options.text: The maximum volume you can do before performance regresses
The maximum weight you can lift once

questions.question: How does 'Junk Volume' affect hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Excessive sets that do not stimulate growth only add fatigue, reducing the quality of subsequent training.

options.text: It increases fatigue without adding stimulus

questions.question: Why is 'standardizing form' critical for tracking progress?
questions.explanation: If form degrades (ROM shortens, momentum used), weight increases are false indicators of strength gains.

options.text: To ensure load increases represent actual strength gains

questions.question: What defines 'Technical Failure'?
questions.explanation: The point where a rep cannot be completed without breaking form. This is the safety limit for compound lifts.

options.text: The point where form breaks down
When the bar speed slows down

questions.question: What is the role of Creatine Monohydrate?
questions.explanation: It saturates muscle phosphocreatine stores, aiding ATP regeneration for high-intensity power output.

options.text: It aids ATP regeneration for power output

questions.question: What is a 'Mesocycle'?
questions.explanation: A block of training (usually 4-8 weeks) focused on a specific adaptation (e.g., Hypertrophy Block).

options.text: A block of training weeks focused on a specific goal

questions.question: How does 'Autoregulation' apply to advanced training?
questions.explanation: Adjusting daily volume/load based on biofeedback (readiness, stress, soreness) rather than a fixed spreadsheet.

options.text: Following the plan exactly no matter what
Adjusting variables based on daily performance/recovery capability

questions.question: What is the 'Moment Arm' in biomechanics?
questions.explanation: The perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (joint) to the line of force. Determines torque (muscle load).

options.text: The distance determining torque/leverage at a joint
How long you hold a rep

questions.question: What is 'Phase Potentiation'?
questions.explanation: Structuring phases so one builds on the next (e.g., Work Capacity -> Hypertrophy -> Strength -> Peaking).

options.text: Using one phase to enhance the results of the next

questions.question: Why might 'Active Insufficiency' matter in exercise selection?
questions.explanation: A muscle cannot generate max tension when fully shortened across two joints (e.g., flexing wrist while flexing fingers).

options.text: It reduces force production in shortened positions
It means the muscle is too weak

questions.question: What is the 'Repeated Bout Effect'?
questions.explanation: The adaptation where muscles become resistant to damage from the same stimulus, reducing DOMS over time.

options.text: Protective adaptation reducing muscle damage from familiar stimuli
Doing the same reps every week

questions.question: How do 'Myo-Reps' or 'Rest-Pause' sets work?
questions.explanation: They bypass the 'junk reps' of a standard set by taking short breaths to keep the muscle in a fatigued state, accumulating effective reps faster.

options.text: They accumulate effective reps near failure with short rests
They use very light weight only

questions.question: What is 'Resensitization'?
questions.explanation: A period of lower volume training (maintenance) to restore the muscle's sensitivity to anabolic signaling (mTOR).

questions.question: Explain 'Regional Hypertrophy'.
questions.explanation: Different exercises can bias growth in specific regions of a muscle (e.g., Lengthened partials for distal quads).

options.text: Non-uniform growth within a muscle based on activation mechanics

questions.question: What is 'Systemic Fatigue' vs 'Local Fatigue'?
questions.explanation: Systemic is central nervous system/whole body drain (e.g., Deadlifts). Local is the specific muscle tiring (e.g., Curls).

options.text: Whole body CNS drain vs Muscle specific drain
